programs.name: MI Health Link
programs.what_it_does: MI Health Link combines Medicare and Medicaid services into a single managed care plan for eligible seniors, streamlining healthcare delivery and reducing out-of-pocket costs. Participants receive all healthcare services, including prescription medications, with no copayments or deductibles for in-network care.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Dual eligible individuals aged 21 or above residing in one of 25 participating counties, with active Medicare Parts A, B, and D, full Medicaid qualification, no hospice care, and not enrolled in MI Choice Waiver or PACE.[1]
programs.benefit_value: No copayments or deductibles for covered services; significant savings on healthcare costs for thousands of participants.[1]

programs.name: MI Choice Medicaid Waiver
programs.what_it_does: The MI Choice Medicaid Waiver provides home and community-based services to help seniors avoid nursing home placement. It includes care management, personal care, homemaking, respite, nursing, and other supports through Area Agencies on Aging.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 55+ meeting Medicaid long-term care criteria, often at risk of nursing facility placement.[2][9]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by need; covers in-home services like personal care, respite, meals, and transportation to support aging in place.[2]

programs.name: Michigan PACE Program
programs.what_it_does: Michigan's Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) delivers comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings. It coordinates all needed care, including adult day health, transportation, and home care, through interdisciplinary teams.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 55+ certified for nursing home level care, able to live safely in the community, in participating areas.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of approved services with no copays for dual eligibles; prevents institutionalization.[1]

programs.name: Medicaid Medicare Premium Payment (QMB, SLMB, QI) Programs
programs.what_it_does: These programs pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors. QMB covers Part A/B premiums and more; SLMB covers Part B; QI offers partial Part B assistance.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+ or disabled, meeting specific income/asset limits (e.g., individual income ≤$2,829/month, assets ≤$2,000 as of 2024).[1]
programs.benefit_value: Pays premiums (e.g., Part B ~$174.70/month in 2024) and cost-sharing; exemptions for home, vehicle, etc.[1]

programs.name: MI Bridges Food Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: Michigan's SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card for purchasing food at approved retailers. It supplements grocery budgets to ensure nutritional needs are met.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+, households with elderly/disabled members; income-based eligibility via MI Bridges.[5][7]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per eligible senior household; maximum varies by household size/income.[5]

programs.name: Michigan Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides cash grants to help pay heating bills and one-time crisis assistance for utility shutoffs. It also funds furnace repairs and weather-related needs.[7]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors; priority for those 60+, disabled, with young children.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Grants $200-1,000+ per household annually, based on income, household size, heating type.[7]

programs.name: Michigan Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Insulates homes, seals leaks, and upgrades heating systems to reduce energy costs and improve safety. Services are free for eligible low-income households.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, disabled, families in Michigan; priority for 60+ and vulnerable groups.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements saving 20-30% on energy bills long-term.[2]

programs.name: Michigan Medicare Assistance Program (MMAP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free, unbiased counseling on Medicare options, including Parts A/B/D, Medigap, and dual eligibility. Counselors help select plans, appeal denials, and maximize savings.[1]
programs.who_its_for: All Michigan Medicare beneficiaries 65+, younger disabled; no income limits.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Saved residents over $800,000 in prescription costs last year; personalized plan optimization.[1]

programs.name: Home Delivered Meals (via Area Agencies on Aging)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Often includes 1-2 meals/day, 5 days/week, with nutrition counseling.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often low-income or at nutritional risk.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Suggested donation ~$3-5/meal; free/low-cost for eligible; serves thousands statewide.[2]

programs.name: Respite Care (MI Choice Waiver & POS)
programs.what_it_does: Provides temporary relief for family caregivers through in-home aides, adult day, or short-term facility stays. Helps prevent caregiver burnout while supporting care recipient safety.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors/disabled enrolled in MI Choice Waiver or AAA services.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Hours/days of paid respite based on care plan; up to 240 hours/year in some programs.[2]

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time paid training at community sites leading to unsubsidized employment. Combines work experience, skills training, and supportive services for older job seekers.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income individuals 55+ in Michigan; income ≤125% poverty level.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage pay (~$7.25-10/hr) for 20 hrs/week; job placement assistance.[6]

programs.name: Legal Assistance for Seniors (AAA-funded)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal advice, representation for benefits, housing, consumer issues, and elder abuse. Operated through Area Agencies on Aging networks.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing legal barriers to services, rights protection.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free consultations/representation; resolves thousands of cases annually.[2]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes, adult foster care, and similar facilities resolving complaints. Protects rights, investigates issues, and promotes quality care.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Residents/families in Michigan long-term care facilities; open to public inquiries.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving 80%+ of complaints without formal action; statewide coverage.[2]

programs.name: Michigan's Choice Waiver Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides home and community-based services as an alternative to nursing home placement for seniors needing extensive care. It allows eligible individuals to receive long-term care support while living at home.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Michigan residents aged 65+ (or 18-64 with disabilities), with monthly income ≤$2,829 and assets ≤$2,000 (exemptions for home under $713,000, vehicle, burial plots).[1]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific Medicaid waiver program tailored to Michigan's criteria, emphasizing alternatives to institutional care with strict asset exemptions unique to MI guidelines.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Home-based long-term care services; financial thresholds: income $2,829/mo, assets $2,000.[1]

programs.name: MI Health Link Program
programs.what_it_does: Integrates Medicare and Medicaid into a single managed care plan for dual-eligible seniors, streamlining healthcare and eliminating copays/deductibles for in-network care including prescriptions. It reduces out-of-pocket costs and simplifies navigation between federal programs.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Age 21+, residing in 25 participating MI counties, with Medicare Parts A/B/D, full Medicaid eligibility, not in hospice, MI Choice, or PACE.[1]
programs.whats_unique: Michigan-specific integrated care model for dual eligibles in designated counties, combining services through state-managed plans not universally replicated.[1]
programs.benefit_value: No copays/deductibles for covered care; serves thousands of MI dual eligibles.[1]

programs.name: MI Choice Program
programs.what_it_does: Medicaid-funded initiative delivering home and community-based services to qualified adults. It supports independent living through a range of long-term care options outside institutions.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Adults 60+ qualifying for Medicaid home and community-based services via AgeWays or similar agencies.[4]
programs.whats_unique: Michigan's branded Medicaid waiver service delivery system, coordinated through state area agencies on aging for personalized home care plans.[1][4]
programs.benefit_value: Comprehensive home and community-based long-term care services.[4]

programs.name: Michigan Senior Property Tax Relief
programs.what_it_does: Provides property tax relief to qualifying seniors on fixed incomes. It reduces housing cost burdens through state-specific exemptions or credits.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors meeting Michigan's property tax relief eligibility, typically 65+ with income/asset limits.[2]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded relief program beyond federal standards, targeted at MI homeowners with unique qualification criteria.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Property tax reductions (specific amounts vary by eligibility and local assessments).[2]

programs.name: MiCAFE (Michigan's Coordinated Access to Food for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Assists seniors in applying for food benefits like SNAP via simplified processes. It enables grocery purchases using Michigan Bridge Cards, overcoming application barriers.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ statewide, including those who thought they had too much income or faced access issues.[6]
programs.whats_unique: Michigan-specific program by Elder Law of Michigan for coordinated food benefit access, serving tens of thousands with state-tailored application help.[6]
programs.benefit_value: $23-$292/month in food benefits per single senior.[6]
